content:
4 min read Just now -- This is one of the most important concepts when learning NgRx Signal Store. | Feature | `signal()` | `signalState()` | | -------------------------- | --------------------- | ------------------------- | | Comes From | Angular | NgRx | | Purpose | Single reactive value | Reactive object state | | Nested Reactivity | No | Yes (Deep Signals) | | Updates | `set()`, `update()` | `patchState()` | | Computed Nested Properties | Manual | Automatic | | Best For | Local UI state | Feature/Application state | | Deep Property Tracking | ❌ | ✅ || Scenario | signal() | signalState() | | ----------------------- | --------------------- | ---------------------------- | | Change `name` | Entire object changes | Only `name` signal changes | | Change `street` | Entire object changes | Only `street` signal changes | | Computed Recalculation | More frequent | More targeted | | Memory Usage | Lower | Slightly higher | | Large State Objects | Less efficient | More efficient | | Fine-Grained Reactivity | ❌ | ✅ | Signal vs SignalState — Update & Computation | Feature | `signal()` | `signalState()` | | ---------------------- | ------------------------- | ---------------------------- | | Package | `@angular/core` | `@ngrx/signals` | | State Type | Single value or object | Structured application state | | Nested Reactivity | ❌ Whole object tracked | ✅ Deep Signals | | Read Value | `person()` | `person.name()` | | Update Value | `set()`, `update()` | `patchState()` | | Computed Value | `computed()` | `computed()` | | Nested Property Access | `person().address.street` | `person.address.street()` | | Update Nested Property | Manual immutable update | `patchState()` | | Best Use Case | UI state | Feature state |Example State interface Person { id: number; name: string; address: { street: string; city: string; }; }1. State Creation Press enter or click to view image in full size 2. Reading Values | signal() | signalState() | | ------------------------------- | ------------------------------- | | `ts
person().name` | `ts
person.name()` | | `ts
person().address.street` | `ts
person.address.street()` |Dependency Tracking signal() --------------------------------------------------------- person() ↓ whole object dependencysignalState() --------------------------------------------------------- person.address.street() ↓ only street dependency 3. Updating Root Property Change Name signal() this.person.update(person => ({ ...person, name: 'Bob' }));signalState() patchState(this.person, { name: 'Bob' });| Comparison | signal() | signalState() | | ------------------------- | -------- | ------------- | | Lines of Code | More | Less | | Immutable Update Required | Yes | No | | Readability | Medium | High |5. Computed Values Full Address signal() readonly fullAddress = computed( () => `${this.person().address.street}, ${this.person().address.city}` );signalState() readonly fullAddress = computed( () => `${this.person.address.street()}, ${this.person.address.city()}` );1. Angular signal() readonly count = signal(0);Read: this.count()Update: this.count.set(10);or this.count.update(v => v + 1);Object with signal readonly person = signal({ id: 1, name: 'John', address: { street: 'Main St' } });Access: this.person().address.streetNotice: person()returns the entire object. Angular treats it as a single signal. person | +-- objectNo separate signal exists for: address street cityUpdating a nested property Must update immutably: this.person.update(person => ({ ...person, address: { ...person.address, street: 'New Street' } }));2. NgRx signalState() readonly person = signalState({ id: 1, name: 'John', address: { street: 'Main St' } });Now NgRx creates Deep Signals. Access: this.person.address.street()Notice: street()because street itself is a signal. Internally: person ├── id ├── name └── address └── streetEach property becomes reactive. signal() person (Signal) | +---- entire objectTo get street: this.person().address.streetsignalState() person ├── id() ├── name() └── address └── street()To get street: this.person.address.street()Why signalState is Powerful Suppose: readonly person = signalState({ id: 1, name: 'John', address: { street: 'Main St', city: 'NY' } });Template: {{ person.address.street() }}Only the street signal is tracked. street changes ↓ only street consumers updateWith regular signal: {{ person().address.street }}Angular tracks: person signalThe whole object dependency. Updating State signal() this.person.update(person => ({ ...person, name: 'Bob' }));signalState() patchState(this.person, { name: 'Bob' });Cleaner. Real Example signal() readonly filters = signal({ search: '', page: 1, pageSize: 20 });Read: this.filters().searchUpdate: this.filters.update(f => ({ ...f, search: 'Angular' }));signalState() readonly filters = signalState({ search: '', page: 1, pageSize: 20 });Read: this.filters.search()Update: patchState(this.filters, { search: 'Angular' });Much cleaner. When to Use signal() Use for simple local state: loading selectedTab theme dialogOpen counter searchTextExample: readonly loading = signal(false); readonly selectedTab = signal(0);When to Use signalState() Use for complex object state: User Profile Dashboard Shopping Cart Orders Products Feature StateExample: readonly userState = signalState({ user: null, loading: false, error: null });In Modern Angular 19/20 A common pattern is: loading = signal(false); filters = signalState({ search: '', page: 1 }); usersStore = inject(UsersStore);Where: • signal() → simple UI flags • signalState() → complex feature state • signalStore() → full-fledged state management with methods, computed values, and RxJS integration Think of it as: signal() ↓ single valuesignalState() ↓ state object with deep signals signalStore() ↓ complete state management solution That’s why most NgRx Signal Store examples internally build on top of signalState, because it provides deep reactive state out of the box.
